bibliographic_data.title: TESS Discovery of a Transiting Super-Earth in the $π$ Mensae System
bibliographic_data.abstract: We report the detection of a transiting planet around π Men (HD 39091), using data from the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS). The solar-type host star is unusually bright (V = 5.7) and was already known to host a Jovian planet on a highly eccentric, 5.7-year orbit. The newly discovered planet has a size of 2.04 ± 0.05 R⊕and an orbital period of 6.27 days. Radial-velocity data from the HARPS and AAT/UCLES archives also displays a 6.27-day periodicity, confirming the existence of the planet and leading to a mass determination of 4.82±0.85 M⊕. The star’s proximity and brightness will facilitate further investigations, such as atmospheric spectroscopy, asteroseismology, the Rossiter–McLaughlin effect, astrometry, and direct imaging.

core_content.novelty_claim: The discovery of a transiting super-Earth around π Men (HD 39091) using TESS data, with confirmation through radial-velocity measurements.
core_content.key_methodology: TESS photometry and archival radial velocity data analysis.
outcomes: Detection of a new transiting planet π Men c
Confirmation of the planet's existence via radial-velocity data
core_content.full_text_markdown: The mission of TESS is to search for transiting planets as small as Earth around the nearest and brightest stars. The spacecraft was launched on April 18, 2018, and began its sky survey on July 25. This paper reports the discovery of a small transiting planet π Men c around a bright star π Men (HD 39091). The TESS photometric data from Sector 1 revealed a signal with a period of 6.27 days, an amplitude of about 300 ppm, and a duration of 3 hours. Radial-velocity data from the HARPS and AAT/UCLES archives confirmed this periodicity, leading to a mass determination of 4.82±0.85 M⊕. The star's proximity and brightness will facilitate further investigations such as atmospheric spectroscopy, asteroseismology, the Rossiter–McLaughlin effect, astrometry, and direct imaging.
